text: The video shows the port placement for a robotic cholecystectomy using the DaVinci XI system, with the robotic arms docked from the right-hand side.

The surgical setup includes the placement of the optical port twenty centimeters away from the target anatomy, Calot's triangle, located five centimeters below the costal margin. The ports are arranged in a linear fashion with eight centimeters separating the working ports from the optical port, and an assistant port is placed eight centimeters laterally.
The surgeon activates the ICG mode, also known as Firefly mode, to visualize the biliary tree, confirming adequate secretion of the dye for enhanced anatomical definition.

The surgeon begins the robotic cholecystectomy by dividing the peritoneum over the neck of the gallbladder. Both the anterior and posterior peritoneal layers are incised, followed by the sequential division of the fibro fatty tissue covering the gallbladder.
The maneuverability of the hook remains a very strong plus point in usage of this instrument over a scissor or a Marylin forceps.

The surgeon encounters the cystic lymph node, known as the lymph node of LUN, near the cystic artery during dissection in the lower part of the gallbladder and Calot's triangle. Careful, gentle dissection is performed, taking small bits of tissue to avoid injury to the cystic duct and artery before their identification.

The robotic surgery utilizes ICG imaging to enhance visual identification of the biliary anatomy. The ICG delineates the biliary tree, providing a clear view of the structures. The cystic lymph node is visible, having been pushed away during the procedure.

The ICG fluorescence highlights the cystic duct, common bile duct, and Hartman's pouch. The surgeon uses the robotic platform to manage the dissection, further delineating the cystic duct. The tissue between the cystic artery and the cystic duct is carefully dissected using a hook instrument.

The surgeon uses the ICG firefly mode to ensure accurate identification of structures, avoiding accessory ducts. The tissues overlying the gallbladder are divided, with dissection extending distally both anteriorly and posteriorly to free the gallbladder from its peritoneal attachments.
The surgeon divides the posterior peritoneal layer and continues to clear tissue near the Calot's triangle region using a hook instrument, which is highlighted as versatile for dissection.

The surgeon navigates around Hartman's pouch using the robotic platform and ICG imaging to maintain the correct anatomical plane. The cystic artery and cystic duct are confirmed visually, with no accessory ducts encountered. The cystic artery is then delineated.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument to cauterize a long segment of tissue, followed by division with a monopolar hook, ensuring thorough cauterization to prevent post-operative bleeding from the stump.

The surgeon focuses on freeing the cystic duct from its posterior attachments, creating a window posterior to the duct for successful clipping. Hemolock clips are applied to the cystic duct, which is triply clipped. The ICG Firefly mode is repeatedly used to delineate the anatomy, ensuring accurate identification of structures. The cystic duct is then divided using scissors.

The surgeon has divided the cystic artery and cystic duct, confirming no accessory or aberrant ducts are present. The gallbladder is now being freed from its attachments and the fibro-fatty tissue at Calot's triangle, followed by lifting it from the liver bed.
The surgeon encounters edema and fluid in the areolar tissue due to inflammation, using these features to identify the correct dissection plane.
The surgeon encounters small blood vessels in the peritoneal fold anteriorly or in the liver bed. Monopolar current is applied for hemostasis, with the option to use bipolar cautery if necessary.

The surgeon applies dynamic traction to the gallbladder, altering its direction to sequentially open tissue planes. Using a monopolar hook, fibrous strands are divided, allowing the gallbladder to be separated from the liver bed with minimal bleeding.

The surgeon carefully completes the dissection and separation of the gallbladder from the liver bed, ensuring no tearing of liver tissue. A final inspection confirms the absence of bleeding or bile leaks, with all clips securely in place.
The gallbladder is placed into an endo bag and extracted from the surgical site following standard procedures.
